title: Log Loss for Evaluation
concept: Log Loss measures how close a model's predicted probabilities are to the actual labels, heavily penalizing confident wrong predictions.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main advantage of using AUC over accuracy to evaluate a classifier?

options.text: AUC measures ranking performance across all thresholds, making it robust against class imbalance.
AUC is calculated much faster than accuracy.
AUC tells you exactly which decision threshold you should use.

exercises.explain: Correct! AUC evaluates the model's overall ranking ability across all thresholds, whereas accuracy only checks a single threshold. Now let's see how we measure probability calibration directly.

cards.title: Confidence matters
cards.body: If a forecaster says there is a 99% chance of rain and it is sunny, you lose trust. If they say there is a 51% chance of rain and it is sunny, you are more forgiving. We need a metric that measures this confidence level.

cards.title: What is Log Loss?
cards.body: Log Loss (logarithmic loss) evaluates the quality of predicted probabilities. Unlike binary metrics (0 or 1), it penalizes predictions based on how far their probabilities are from the true labels.

cards.title: The betting penalty
cards.body: Think of Log Loss as a penalty in a game of bets. If you bet $99 on 'Yes' and 'No' happens, you pay a massive fine. If you bet only $51 on 'Yes' and 'No' happens, your fine is much smaller.

cards.title: Penalizing confident errors
cards.body: If a transaction is fraud (1): predicting 0.99 fraud probability gives a tiny log loss. Predicting 0.51 gives a moderate log loss. Predicting 0.01 (confident error) triggers an extremely high log loss.

cards.title: Does low error mean calibration?
cards.body: A model can have 90% accuracy but terrible calibration if its probabilities are always clustered near 0.5 or extreme 0.99. Log Loss forces the model to output mathematically reliable probabilities.

cards.title: Log Loss Penalty Scale
cards.body: How log loss penalizes predictions when the actual label is 1 (True):

left.text:
Close to 1.0.
Log Loss is very low (approx. 0.10).

right.text:
Confident error.
Log Loss is extremely high (approx. 4.60).

exercises.prompt: For a classification model, is a lower Log Loss or a higher Log Loss better?

options.text: Lower Log Loss, because it represents smaller errors and better calibration.
Higher Log Loss, because it indicates the model is learning more patterns.
It depends on whether we want high precision or high recall.

exercises.explain: Log Loss is a cost/loss metric, so lower values mean the predicted probabilities are closer to the true labels (better performance).

exercises.prompt: True or false: Log Loss only depends on whether the final predicted label (after thresholding) is correct, not on the raw probability.
exercises.explain: False. Log Loss directly evaluates the raw predicted probability (e.g., 0.85 vs 0.55), penalizing how wrong the model's confidence was.

exercises.sentence: A model that predicts 0.99 probability for a positive case that turns out to be negative will receive a very ___ log loss penalty.
exercises.explain: Because the model was highly confident (0.99) but completely wrong, the logarithmic penalty is extremely large.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following prediction probabilities for a positive class (actual label = 1) yields the lowest (best) log loss?
exercises.explain: A probability of 0.95 is closest to the true label of 1, resulting in the smallest penalty (lowest log loss).

exercises.prompt: You are training a self-driving car model to detect pedestrians. Model X predicts 0.90 probability and is correct 90% of the time, but when it misses, it predicted 0.01 probability. Model Y is also correct 90% of the time, but when it misses, its prediction was 0.45. Which model has a better (lower) Log Loss and why?

options.text: Model Y, because it is less confidently wrong when it makes mistakes, leading to a much lower log loss.
Model X, because predicting 0.01 is more decisive than predicting 0.45.
Both have the same Log Loss because their overall accuracy is 90%.
Model X, because 0.90 is closer to 1.0.

exercises.explain: Model Y is much less confidently wrong. Predicting 0.01 for a pedestrian who actually exists creates a huge logarithmic penalty, making Model X's Log Loss much worse. 🎉
